A1-epa-pair:
## The governing law: Act 1124 of 2025

The statute now governing environmental protection of your mining operations is the **Environmental Protection Act, 2025 (Act 1124)** — not the 1994 Act. The 1994 Act 490 still surfaces in the older mining-policy materials as the operative environmental statute, but as a matter of currency that reading is out of date.The Environmental Protection Act, 2025 (Act 1124) repeals and replaces the Environmental Protection Agency Act, 1994 (Act 490).The live instrument is therefore Act 1124, and Act 490 now serves only to explain the position that preceded it. 

## How the law got there: the diachronic path

**The starting point (1994–2024).** Act 490 was the consolidating environmental statute. It was itself a consolidation exercise —an Act to amend and consolidate the law relating to environmental protection, pesticides control and regulation and for related purposes— and it established the regulator as the Environmental Protection *Agency*,a body corporate with perpetual succession and a common seal that may sue and be sued in its corporate name.This is the Act the Minerals and Mining Policy still references: it treats environmental standards for mining ascontained in the National Environmental Policy of Ghana, complemented by the Environmental Protection Agency Act, 1994 (Act 490) and the Environmental Assessment Regulations, 1999 (L.I. 1652).That policy statement, and the standard Mining Lease clause requiring approvals from "the Environmental Protection Agency," reflect the *former* regime.

**What changed it (January 2025).** Act 1124 was passed andassented to on 6th January, 2025.Its long title makes plain that it does the same consolidating work Act 490 once did, and more:it is an Act to amend and consolidate laws relating to environmental protection; establish the Environmental Protection Authority to regulate and protect the environment, provide for pesticide control and regulation; provide for the control, management and disposal of hazardous waste and electrical and electronic waste; provide for the co-ordination of climate change responses and for related matters.A later comprehensive re-enactment of the same subject-matter supersedes its predecessor — *lex posterior derogat priori* — and here the supersession is express, not merely implied.

**What it is now.** The regulator is no longer the Agency but the **Authority**.Act 1124 establishes the Environmental Protection Authority as a body corporate.Its remit is deliberately broader than the old Agency's; its object isto regulate, protect, co-ordinate and exercise general oversight over all matters relating to climate change and the environment.The addition of climate change and a dedicated hazardous- and electronic-waste regime is the substantive expansion that the 2006-era mining policy simply did not contemplate.

## The transition provisions — why this is seamless for you, not disruptive

The practically important point for a mining operator is that the change of statute does **not** reopen your existing arrangements. Act 1124 carries the old Agency's legal life across to the new Authority in three respects:

- **Litigation and legal proceedings continue unbroken.**Proceedings taken by or against the Environmental Protection Agency may be continued by or against the Environmental Protection Authority.- **Existing contracts survive.**A contract subsisting between the former Agency and any other person and in effect immediately before the coming into force of the Act shall subsist between the Authority and that other person.So any environmental undertaking, permit condition or agreement your company holds with the former Agency now runs against the Authority automatically.
- **Staff transfer on protected terms.**A person employed by the former Agency immediately before the Act came into force is deemed duly employed by the Authority on terms not less favourable in aggregate to those attached to the post before commencement.The upshot: the institutional identity changed, but the continuity clauses mean you do not need to re-apply, re-contract or re-litigate merely because the statute changed. The permits and the regulatory relationship carry over.

## Reading the old references correctly

Two of your governing mining documents still name the old regulator, and both must now be read as pointing to Act 1124. The standard Mining Lease requires the company, before undertaking operations, toobtain the necessary approvals and permits as may be required by law from the Forestry Commission, Water Resources Commission and the Environmental Protection Agency or any other Regulatory body.Likewise the Minerals and Mining Policy assigns environmental regulation of mining tothe Environmental Protection Agency (EPA), the Ministry responsible for the environment, the Forestry Commission and the Water Resources Commission.On the ordinary interpretive rule that a reference to a repealed enactment (or to a body dissolved and re-established under it) is read as a reference to its statutory successor, every such reference to the "Environmental Protection Agency" is now a reference to the **Environmental Protection Authority** under Act 1124. The lease's own "or any other law from time to time" framing reinforces this: your compliance obligation attaches to whatever the environmental statute in force requires, and that statute is Act 1124.

It is worth being candid about one limit. Your underlying **mining** law regime — the Minerals and Mining Act, 2006 (Act 703), its 2019 amendment, and the L.I. 2173/2175/2176 regulations — is untouched by this change; Act 1124 replaces the *environmental* statute, not the mining code. The two operate in parallel:mineral rights cannot be granted unless the applicant provides a satisfactory programme of environmental protection measures and how these are funded, and mining companies must comply with environmental regulations, including environmental impact assessment for environmental permitting.Those requirements remain, but the environmental permitting and EIA process they cross-refer to is now administered under Act 1124.

## Bottom line

Apply **Act 1124 (2025)** as the governing environmental statute; deal with the **Environmental Protection Authority**, not the former Agency; and treat every "EPA/Environmental Protection Agency" reference in your lease and in the mining policy as reading through to the Authority under Act 1124. Act 490 is now relevant only to explain the prior position and to interpret the continuity of anything you established before January 2025 — which, by force of the transitional clauses, carries over intact.

One practical next step worth confirming from Act 1124's own commencement and repeal provisions: the exact date the Act came into force (assent was 6 January 2025, but commencement can differ), since the transitional clauses key protected staff, contracts and proceedings to "immediately before the coming into force of this Act."
A2-703-amend-chain:
Yes — Act 703 has been amended several times since its 2006 enactment, and section 81 in particular has been through a specific and consequential change. 

## The parent Act and its purpose

Act 703 began life in 2006 as a consolidating statute —"AN ACT to revise and consolidate the law relating to minerals and mining and to provide for connected purposes"— assented to on22nd March 2006. Its foundational norm is constitutional:every mineral in its natural state in Ghana is "the property of the Republic and is vested in the President in trust for the people of Ghana."That vesting is the source from which the whole licensing and offences architecture descends, and it is worth keeping in view because the amendments have progressively sharpened the State's grip on that trust — particularly against illegal and foreign-controlled small-scale mining.

## The amendment history

The materials disclose at least three amending Acts acting on Act 703, in sequence:

**Act 794 (2010)** — This is now spent.The Minerals and Mining (Amendment) Act, 2010 (Act 794) is repealedby the 2015 Amendment, though the pre-existing royalty rate was preserved:"Despite the repeal of Act 794, the rate of royalty in force immediately before the commencement of this Act shall continue in force until the rate is altered."**Act 900 (2015)** — Assented to on16th December 2015, this Act was directed at royalties and illegal small-scale mining.It amended sections 25, 99, 106, 107 and 110 of Act 703 and dealt with repeal and savings.It recast section 25 to make royalty payable"at the rate and in the manner that may be prescribed,"and — importantly for enforcement against galamsey — it built in seizure and forfeiture machinery, requiring a convicting court to"order the forfeiture of any equipment or product seized under subsection (5) to the State,"with the Minister then obliged toallocate the confiscated equipment or product to the appropriate state institution within sixty days and gazette the recipient institution.**Act 995 (2019)** — Assented to on19th August 2019, this Act was aimed squarely at penalties and foreign involvement.It amended section 81, inserted a new section 96A, and amended section 99in orderto increase the penalties for buying or selling minerals without a licence or valid authority, and for mining contrary to the Act.**Act 1140 (2025) — the Ghana Gold Board Act** — This is the most recent instrument acting on Act 703, and it partially carves the statute up as regards gold.It repeals sections 6, 97 and 104 of Act 703 "as pertains to gold,"alongside the Precious Minerals Marketing Corporation Act. The carve-out is not total:the repeal does not affect the power of the Minister responsible for Mines to grant a licence to a large-scale mining company to purchase and deal in gold or other minerals.And it asserts primacy over inconsistent law, providing thatexisting enactments relevant to the Act "shall have effect subject to the modifications necessary to give effect to this Act."So the current form of Act 703 is the 2006 consolidation as modified by Acts 900 (2015) and 995 (2019), with a gold-specific overlay from Act 1140 (2025); Act 794 (2010) is dead.

## Section 81 specifically

This is where the diachronic reading matters most, because section 81 was materially rewritten and its rewrite has a knock-on effect.

**As originally enacted**, section 81 was a scope provision. Its side-note was"Application," and the section read that "Section 82 to 99 apply to small scale mining only."In other words, the entire small-scale-mining Part (licensing under s.82, qualification under s.83, and so on through the offences in s.99) was ring-fenced to small-scale operations.

**Act 995 (2019) substituted a new section 81.**The 2019 Act, treating Act 703 as the "principal enactment," effected "the substitution for section 81"of a two-limbed provision:

>"Application. 81. (1) Sections 82 to 98 apply to small scale mining only. (2) Section 99 applies to illegal small scale mining and other forms of illegal mining."The change is small on the page but significant in effect. The original ring-fenced sections 82–**99** to small-scale mining. The new section 81 splits that block: sections 82–**98** remain confined to small-scale mining, but **section 99 is deliberately cut loose from that confinement** and re-directed at"illegal small scale mining and other forms of illegal mining."The better reading is that Parliament wanted the section 99 offences-and-penalties regime to reach *all* illegal mining, not merely illegal *small-scale* mining — closing an argument that a large-scale illegal operator fell outside section 99 because that section only "applied to small scale mining."

That reading is confirmed by what Act 995 simultaneously did to section 99 itself. The substituted section 99 is not a small-scale-only provision: it penalisesanyone who buys or sells minerals without a licence under section 6, 82, 97 or 104 or without a valid authority, and separately targets"a non-Ghanaian who undertakes a mining operation or facilitates the participation of any person in mining contrary to a provision of this Act."A section reaching non-Ghanaians and dealings licensed under the general sections 6, 97 and 104 plainly could not sensibly remain shackled to "small scale mining only" — so the section 81 amendment and the section 99 substitution are two halves of one legislative move.

Read alongside the new section 96A —inserted by Act 995 to provide that "A non-Ghanaian or a foreign company shall not provide mining support services for a small-scale mining operation"— and the heavy new penalties in section 99 (including, for a non-Ghanaian mining unlawfully,a fine of not less than one hundred thousand and up to three hundred and fifty thousand penalty units and imprisonment of twenty to twenty-five years), the 2019 package reveals its true target: foreign penetration of, and illegality within, the small-scale sector reserved to Ghanaians.

## A caution on the section 99 cross-reference

One live drafting wrinkle is worth flagging. The arrangement of sections in the 2006 text still describes section 99 by reference to the block it once governed —"Offences and penalties under sections 81-99"— and section 82 continues to refer, in the original text, to sections "82 to 99" applying to small-scale mining. After Act 995, the operative section 81 confines only sections 82–98 to small-scale mining. On the ordinary interpretive principle that a live text is read to give effect to the latest amendment, the substituted section 81 governs: **section 99 now applies to illegal mining generally**, and the older marginal cross-references should be read subject to the 2019 substitution rather than as limiting it.

## Bottom line

Act 703 has been amended by Act 900 (2015) and Act 995 (2019), with Act 794 (2010) repealed and a gold-specific carve-out effected by Act 1140 (2025). On section 81, the current form — the version substituted by Act 995 — no longer ring-fences the whole of sections 82–99 to small-scale mining; it confines sections 82–98 to small-scale mining while extending section 99's offences-and-penalties regime to *all* illegal mining. To be certain of the precise current wording of the substituted sections 81 and 99, and of the exact scope of the Act 1140 gold repeals, confirm against the official gazetted versions of Acts 995 and 1140, since the underlying texts here are flagged as OCR extractions to be verified.
A3-gold-board:
**Short answer: no.** A small-scale miner may now sell gold only into the channel the Ghana Gold Board controls. The old world — in which the Minister for Mines licensed any number of private buyers under the Minerals and Mining Act, 2006, and a miner could sell to whichever of them offered the best price — has been closed off by the Ghana Gold Board Act, 2025 (Act 1140). What follows traces how the law moved from the one position to the other, and where it now stands.

**The former position under Act 703.** Gold buying used to run through a licensing power vested in the Minister. Under the Minerals and Mining Act, 2006,the Minister, in consultation with the Commission, could in writing licence persons the Minister considered fit to buy and deal in the types and forms of minerals under terms and conditions specified in the licence.That provision contemplated a plurality of private licensed buyers. A small-scale miner could therefore sell to any of them; the buy-side of the market was open to anyone the Minister was prepared to license. This is the regime the Minerals and Mining Policy itself describes when it notes that, alongside the various mineral rights,specific licences are required also to enable a person to buy and deal in minerals.**What changed it — Act 1140.** The 2025 Act does two things that together dismantle that open buy-side. 

First, it creates a single statutory monopsony over the miner's output.Despite a provision in any other enactment to the contrary, the Gold Board has the sole authority to grade, assay, weigh and value gold produced in, brought into or exported from the country; to purchase and sell gold produced by miners other than large-scale miners; and to export gold produced by a mining company other than a large-scale mining company.The "despite … any other enactment" formula is decisive: it is an express override that displaces the Minister's old licensing power in Act 703 to the extent of any inconsistency. Small-scale miners are precisely the "miners other than large-scale miners" caught by paragraph (b), so their gold is now, by statute, GoldBod's to buy.

Second, the Act extinguishes the very licences on which the old private buyers stood.A licence to purchase and deal in gold issued by the Minister responsible for Mines to a person other than a large scale mining company shall, upon the coming into force of this Act, cease to be valid.The former buyers do not simply lose priority — their authority to buy evaporates by operation of law. Their only route back into the trade is toapply to the Gold Board for a licence under this Act.That the repeal was deliberate and comprehensive is confirmed on the record: the verified updates note thatAct 1140 gives GoldBod the exclusive right to buy, sell, assay, value and export gold and repeals the prior PMMC-based gold marketing/export arrangements.**The current position, applied to the miner.** Put the two limbs together. A small-scale miner can no longer sell to "any person holding a licence to buy gold," because (i) the general ministerial buying licences that used to authorise those persons have ceased to be valid, and (ii) exclusive authority to purchase a non-large-scale miner's gold now vests in GoldBod. Reinforcing the point on the miner's own side of the transaction, the Act makes it an offence tobuy gold from a person in the country unless that person has been issued with a licence by the Gold Board— so even a would-be buyer must now hold a GoldBod licence, not a ministerial one.

The practical consequence is that the miner sells either directly to GoldBod or to an intermediary that GoldBod has itself licensed under the new categories — for example aBuying Licence or an Aggregation Licence,aggregation being defined asthe process of collecting gold from small-scale producers, licensed traders or miners for onward delivery to the Gold Board.Selling outside that chain is not a mere regulatory irregularity.A person who engages in a business or other related activity under this section without a licence commits an offence and is liable on summary conviction to a fine of not less than fifty thousand penalty units and not more than two hundred thousand penalty units or to a term of imprisonment of not less than five years and not more than ten years or to both.**Why the newer Act governs.** The two statutes cover the same ground — who may buy a small-scale miner's gold — and the later one prevails. This is not left to the ordinary presumption that a later, comprehensive re-enactment supersedes the earlier (*lex posterior derogat priori*); Act 1140 says so expressly through its "despite any other enactment to the contrary" clause and its provision voiding the old ministerial licences. Act 703's section 104 is therefore best read as spent so far as gold from non-large-scale miners is concerned; it survives only for minerals and dealings that Act 1140 does not capture.

**One boundary worth flagging.** The monopsony is drawn around *non-large-scale* producers. GoldBod's exclusive purchasing and export authority bites on gold "produced by miners other than large-scale miners," and correspondingly the licence-cancellation in Act 1140 spares "a large scale mining company." Large-scale companies remain on a different footing — GoldBod's dealings with them run instead through the pre-emption route, buyinga portion or all of the gold produced by large scale mining companies in line with the pre-emption rights of Government exercised by the Minister responsible for Mines under section 7 of the Minerals and Mining Act, 2006.For the small-scale miner in the question, though, the channel is now single, statutory, and mandatory.
A4-1972-55pct:
No — the 55% compulsory State stake is dead law. The current rule is a 10% free carried interest under the Minerals and Mining Act, 2006 (Act 703), and understanding how the law travelled from one to the other is the whole point.

**What the position was.** Ghana's mining sector was, on its own account, "to a large extent state controlled between 1957 and 1983." The 55% requirement came out of that era.In 1972, under a plan described as "taking over the commanding heights of the economy," the government, by the Mining Operations (Government Participation) Decree, 1972 (NRCD 132), acquired majority shares (55%) in all the foreign mining companies.The policy record confirms the mechanism at scale:in 1972 the state acquired 55% interest in the non-state owned mining operations.The aim was frankly interventionist —to protect the employment of Ghanaians and to secure foreign currency generated by the mines, with the policy aimed at maximising government revenue, control of resources and employment generation.**What changed it.** The compulsory-participation regime was expressly dismantled, and then the governing statute was itself replaced. Act 703 lists, among the enactments it repeals,the Minerals and Mining Law 1986 (PNDCL 153) and the Mining Operations (Government Participation) (Repeal) Act 1993 (Act 465).That is the audit trail: the 1972 decree's participation scheme had already been undone by the 1993 repeal Act, and Act 703 then swept up the surrounding legislative furniture when it comprehensively re-enacted the field in 2006.

**What the law is now.** The live rule on State participation is section 43 of Act 703, and it is a different animal entirely.Where a mineral right is for mining or exploitation, the Government shall acquire a ten percent free carried interest in the rights and obligations of the mineral operations, in respect of which no financial contribution shall be paid by Government.Two features matter. The stake is 10%, not 55%; and it is a *free carried* interest — the State contributes no capital for it — rather than a compulsory buy-out of a majority. The distance travelled is the point: from a controlling 55% acquisition to a non-contributory 10% carry.

Crucially, the compulsion has gone from the mandatory element and moved into the optional one. Beyond the automatic 10%, further State participation is now consensual, not imposed:subsection (1) does not preclude the Government from any other or further participation in mineral operations that may be agreed with the holder.So any equity above 10% today is a matter for negotiation and agreement with the company — the polar opposite of the 1972 decree, which simply expropriated 55% by operation of law.

**Why the answer is a clean "no," not a hedge.** One might object that the Government retains coercive shareholding tools, so the old interventionism survives in substance. It does not, and the tools that remain prove the shift rather than undercut it. What Act 703 preserves is a narrow "special share" mechanism — a golden share thatmay only be issued to, held by or transferred to the President, the Minister or another person acting on behalf of the Republic, and which confers no right to participate in the dividends, profits or assets of the company.That is a veto/oversight instrument, not an equity grab; it deliberately carries no economic stake. The modern posture toward foreign capital is protective, not extractive: a holder isguaranteed free transferability of convertible currency,and disputes with a foreign holder are routed toinvestment-treaty frameworks or, failing that, UNCITRAL arbitration.A regime that guarantees profit repatriation and offers treaty arbitration is not one that still forces majority State ownership on foreign miners.

**The trajectory confirms the direction.** The move away from compulsory majority control was itself part of a deliberate policy turn —the 1983 Economic Recovery Programme, whose elements included letting exporters retain foreign-currency earnings and disposing of loss-making state enterprises, identified mining as a key sector to revive the economy.The whole legislative arc since then has run in one direction: liberalisation to attract private and foreign investment, not renationalisation.

So, applying this to a foreign mining company today: the 1972 decree's 55% requirement has no application whatsoever. The company's exposure to State equity is confined to the automatic 10% free carried interest under section 43(1), plus whatever further participation it *agrees* to under section 43(2). Cite the 1972 decree only to measure how far the law has moved — never as a subsisting obligation.

One caveat on scope. For gold specifically, the recent Ghana Gold Board Act, 2025 (Act 1140) has begun re-carving parts of Act 703 — itrepeals sections 6, 97 and 104 of Act 703 as they pertain to gold.Section 43 is not among the provisions touched, so the 10%-carry rule stands, but a gold-sector answer should confirm the current section 43 position against Act 1140's marketing and dealing regime before relying on it wholesale.
B1-companies-act:
The requirement itself is straightforward on the face of the Act.Section 10 of Act 703 provides that, unless otherwise provided in the Act, a mineral right shall not be granted to a person unless the person is a body incorporated under the Companies Code 1963 (Act 179), under the Incorporated Private Partnerships Act 1962 (Act 152) or under an enactment in force.The same cross-reference to Act 179 runs through the associated instruments: the Licensing Regulations require an applicant to producecertified copies of documents of incorporation showing that the applicant is a body incorporated under the Companies Act, 1963 (Act 179) or the Incorporated Private Partnership Act, 1962 (Act 152) or registered under any other enactment in force, and the Commission's review checks thatthe applicant is a body incorporated under the Companies Act, 1963 (Act 179) or the Incorporated Private Partnership Act, 1962 (Act 152) or registered under any other enactment in force.But a statutory reference must be read as the law now stands, not as it stood when the words were enacted. Act 703 dates from 2006 —its date of assent was 22nd March 2006— and the Companies Code 1963 (Act 179) it names has since been replaced. The company statute governing incorporation in Ghana today is the **Companies Act, 2019 (Act 992)**, which repealed and re-enacted the 1963 Code. So although section 10 still speaks of "the Companies Code 1963 (Act 179)", the incorporation it demands is now effected under Act 992.

The mechanism that carries the old words forward is the ordinary rule of statutory interpretation that a reference in one enactment to a repealed enactment is read as a reference to the enactment that has replaced it (in Ghana, this is the effect of the Interpretation Act, 2009 (Act 792)). On that rule, section 10's phrase "incorporated under the Companies Code 1963 (Act 179)" is now read as "incorporated under the Companies Act, 2019 (Act 992)". The reference does not become inoperative merely because Parliament has not gone back to amend the words of Act 703; it re-points automatically to the successor statute.

Two points of honest scope. First, the specific provision *of Act 992* under which a company is incorporated is a matter of the current Companies Act, and that instrument is not among the passages before me — the incorporation and certification provisions sit in the early Part of Act 992, and the exact section should be confirmed against the Act itself rather than pinned to a number here. What I can state with confidence is the identity of the governing statute (Act 992) and the interpretive route by which section 10 of Act 703 now reaches it. Second, the materials here confirm the *old* reference is a repealed one only indirectly — Act 703's own repeal clause repeals a list of mining enactments, not the Companies Code — so the replacement of Act 179 by Act 992 rests on the general legal record of Ghana's company-law reform rather than on any repeal clause in these documents.

The practical consequence for a mineral-right applicant is simply this: to satisfy section 10 today, it incorporates under the Companies Act, 2019 (Act 992) and produces its Act 992 incorporation documents to the Commission — and that filing discharges the section 10 and Regulation requirements notwithstanding their continued textual reference to "Act 179". The wording is legacy; the obligation is current.
B2-external-company: The definition you are looking for sits in **section 302 of the Companies Code, 1963 (Act 179)**. On its terms,an external company is a body corporate formed outside Ghana which, at or subsequently to, the commencement of this Code has an established place of business in Ghana.Two points sharpen this. First, the concept turns on an "established place of business," which the section defines broadly:the expression "established place of business" means a branch, management, share, transfer, or registration office, factory, mine, or other fixed place of business, but does not include an agency unless the agent has, and habitually exercises, a general authority to negotiate and conclude contracts on behalf of the body corporate or maintains a stock of merchandise belonging to that body corporate from which he regularly fills orders on its behalf.The Code then carves out two situations that do NOT create such a place:a body corporate is not deemed to have an established place of business merely because it carries on business dealings through a bona fide broker or general commission agent acting in the ordinary course of his business, and the fact that it has a subsidiary incorporated, resident, or carrying on business in Ghana does not of itself constitute the place of business of that body corporate.Second, the Code's own interpretation section confirms that section 302 is the operative definitional home:"external company" shall have the meaning assigned to it by section 302 of this Code.**A caveat on currency you should confirm.** The question asks for "the company law currently in force." The instrument in front of me is the Companies Code, 1963 (Act 179), described as "as amended," and I can see it was still being amended into the 1990s — for instance,subsection (2a) of section 314 was inserted by the Companies Code (Amendment) Act, 1994 (Act 474) s. 6.On that footing, section 302 of Act 179 is the provision to cite for the definition. Ghana's companies legislation has since moved on to a re-enacted statute, and the ordinary interpretive rule is that a reference to a repealed enactment reads as a reference to its successor; the safest formulation is therefore to cite **section 302 of Act 179** as the definition on the materials here, while confirming the equivalent section number in the current re-enacting Act before you rely on it in a live matter.
C1-1140-current:
As of the vetted position recorded in these materials, Act 1140 is in force and is itself the repealing instrument — not the repealed one.

The Act received presidential assent on2nd April, 2025, and was gazetted the same day —the date of Gazette notification is 2nd April, 2025. A human-checked legal update confirms its currency directly:as of the check on 2026-07-08, the Gold Board Act, 2025 (Act 1140) repeals/replaces PMMC's former statutory gold-marketing and export-licensing role. That entry is expressly flagged as the reliable one —each line was checked by a person against the primary source and recorded as CONFIRMED or DENIED, and these are to be treated as the vetted position that overrides an unverified web scan.

So the direction of legal travel runs the other way from the question's suggestion. Act 1140 does the repealing.Act 1140 gives GoldBod the exclusive right to buy, sell, assay, value and export gold and repeals the prior PMMC-based gold marketing/export arrangements. The Act's own text bears this out through its repeal, transitional and precedence machinery. Its transitional provisions absorb the predecessor body wholesale:the rights, assets and liabilities that had accrued in respect of the properties vested in the Precious Minerals Marketing Company immediately before the Act came into force are transferred to the Gold Board, existing PMMC staff are deemed re-employed by the Gold Board, anda contract subsisting between the PMMC and another person in effect immediately before commencement continues between the Gold Board and that person subject to necessary modifications. Old dealing licences fall away —a licence to purchase and deal in gold issued by the Minister responsible for Mines to a person other than a large-scale mining company ceases to be valid on the coming into force of the Act.

Crucially, Act 1140 also arms itself against later or competing legislation through a self-executing priority clause:where there is a conflict or inconsistency between the provisions of this Act and any other enactment relevant to it, the provisions of this Act prevail. That, together with the "despite any other enactment" language granting the Board its exclusive functions, means that even a later general statute touching the same ground would not silently displace Act 1140 — it would take an express amendment or repeal to do so, and the materials show none.

A word on confidence and on how these sources should be read. The confirmation of currency rests on a human-verified update entry rather than on my having the repeal instrument's own text in front of me here; the entry itself points to the repeals/savings section of the official Act as where it was checked. That is a sound basis to state the Act is in force, and I would put the confidence high. If you need to cite the repeal provision by number, the arrangement of sections locates it —section 75 deals with repeal, revocation and savings— and that is the provision to quote for the precise wording of what Act 1140 repealed.

One caveat worth flagging for completeness. The two Minerals and Mining amendment instruments in the materials (Act 900 of 2015 and Act 995 of 2019) amend the *Minerals and Mining Act, 2006 (Act 703)*, a different statute; they do not touch Act 1140 and say nothing about its currency. Nothing in the corpus records any Act later than 1140 amending or repealing it. On the vetted record, then, the Ghana Gold Board Act, 2025 stands in force as the operative — and repealing — statute.
C2-703-principal:
Act 703 remains Ghana's principal minerals and mining statute. It has been amended repeatedly, and a slice of its subject matter (gold marketing and export) has been carved out by a 2025 statute — but nothing in the materials replaces it wholesale. 

**The foundation and the principal Act.** The starting point is constitutional:mineral resources belong to the Ghanaian people in common, and their stewardship is entrusted to the President in accordance with the governing provisions of the Constitution.Act 703 gives that vesting statutory expression —every mineral in its natural state in, under or upon land in Ghana... is the property of the Republic and is vested in the President in trust for the people of Ghana.Act 703 was itself a replacement Act.Government developed a new mining law, Minerals and Mining Act, 2006 (Act 703) to replace the Minerals and Mining Law, 1986 (PNDCL 153).Its long title captures its role:an Act to revise and consolidate the law relating to minerals and mining and to provide for connected purposes.That word "consolidate" matters — Act 703 is the comprehensive backbone regime, running from ownership and the cadastral system through reconnaissance, prospecting and mining leases to royalties, offences and administration.

**How the law has moved since 2006 — amendment, not replacement.** The correct way to read the corpus is diachronically: later statutes have acted *on* Act 703 as its principal enactment, not swept it away. Three amendment Acts confirm this precisely because each one expressly treats Act 703 as "the principal enactment" and edits named sections of it:

- The 2015 Act wasan Act to amend the Minerals and Mining Act, 2006 (Act 703) to provide for Regulations to be made to prescribe the manner for the payment of royalties; the confiscation of equipment used in illegal small scale mining and for related matters.It worked by substituting individual sections — for instance,substituting for section 25 a new royalties provision under which a holder of a mining lease, restricted mining lease or small scale mining lease shall pay royalty to the Republic at the rate and in the manner that may be prescribed.- The 2019 Act was likewisean Act to amend the Minerals and Mining Act, 2006 (Act 703) to increase the penalties for a person who buys or sells minerals without a licence... and to provide for related matters.It amended section 81, inserted a new section 96A, and substituted section 99 — including the new prohibition thata non-Ghanaian or a foreign company shall not provide mining support services for a small-scale mining operation.An amending Act that identifies Act 703 as "the principal enactment" and substitutes discrete sections is the clearest possible signal that Act 703 survives as the governing statute — the amendments live *inside* it. Read Act 703 today, therefore, as the 2006 text with sections 25, 81, 96A, 99, 106, 107 and 110 in their amended form.

**The 2025 development — a carve-out, not a new principal Act.** The one genuinely new statute is the Ghana Gold Board Act, 2025 (Act 1140). This is where care is needed, because it is easy to mistake a subject-specific reform for a replacement of the principal Act. It is not. Act 1140 is institutional and commodity-specific:an Act to establish the Ghana Gold Board to oversee, monitor and undertake the buying, selling and export of gold and other precious minerals... and provide for related matters.What it repeals is narrow and surgical.The repealed provisions are the Precious Minerals Marketing Corporation Act, 1989 (PNDCL 219); and sections 6, 97 and 104 of the Minerals and Mining Act, 2006 (Act 703) as pertains to gold.Three points follow. First, the repeal of parts of Act 703 is confined to the *gold* dimension of the three marketing/export/dealing sections — it does not touch ownership, mineral rights, leases, royalties or the administrative architecture. Second, the repeal is expressly qualified:it shall not affect the power of the Minister responsible for Mines to grant a licence to a large-scale mining company to purchase and deal in gold or other minerals.Third, and tellingly, the Gold Board Act continues to define and rely on Act 703's institutions — it defines the Minerals Commission by reference tothe Minerals Commission established under section 1 of the Minerals Commission Act, 1993 (Act 450).A statute that leans on the machinery of the existing regime is supplementing it, not supplanting it.

Act 1140 does contain a strong priority clause —where there is a conflict or inconsistency between the provisions of this Act and any other enactment relevant to this Act, the provisions of this Act shall prevail.But a "this Act prevails on conflict" clause is a coordination rule for overlap, not a wholesale repeal of Act 703; on its own terms it operates only where there is actual inconsistency, and the express savings above show the drafters intended Act 703 to keep running everywhere else.

**The better view.** Act 703, as amended in 2015 and 2019, is still Ghana's principal minerals and mining statute. It has *not* been replaced by a new principal Act. What has happened is a familiar pattern in a maturing regime: the core consolidating Act endures, successive amendment Acts refine it in place, and a 2025 institutional statute has carved gold *marketing, purchase and export* out of Act 703's sections 6, 97 and 104 and vested that function in a new Gold Board — while leaving the ownership, licensing, leasing, royalty and administrative spine of Act 703 fully intact. The Minerals Commission Act, 1993 (Act 450), which establishes the regulator responsible forthe regulation and management of the utilization of the mineral resources of Ghana and the co-ordination of the policies in relation to them,continues to sit alongside it unchanged.

In practical terms: for any mineral other than gold, and for every non-marketing question even as to gold (grant of rights, leases, royalties, offences), Act 703 governs. For the buying, selling, valuing and exporting of gold, read Act 703 as pro tanto displaced by Act 1140. One targeted next step would be to confirm the current consolidated text of sections 6, 97 and 104 against the official published Act to see exactly how the "as pertains to gold" carve-out has been rendered on the face of Act 703.
